text: The surgeon begins with a 3 cm port in the anterior axillary line at the fifth intercostal space. The incision is extended in the intercostal space, allowing the scope to be advanced to the uppermost part of the port. A lanky rasper is inserted, revealing a lung lesion and a complete fissure. The lung is retracted cranially to expose the inferior pulmonary ligament. Using an energy sealing device and suction, dissection is performed with counter traction provided by the suction, while a secondary assistant uses lung grasping forceps to retract the lung.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to divide adhesions and ligaments, facilitating dissection around the inferior pulmonary vein. The assistant retracts the lung using lung parenchymal forceps. The surgeon employs a gyroscopic right angle clamp to encircle the inferior pulmonary vein, then clears extra adhesions with the harmonic to prepare space for the stapler. The dissection continues through the fissure to expose the common basilar and superior segmental arterial branches.

The surgeon continues the dissection using Damego forceps and suction, maneuvering both hands with advanced instrumentation. The right angle clamp is used to encircle the artery, followed by the application of a curved tip powered vascular stapler to divide the anterior branch. The focus then shifts back to the vein.

The surgeon places the suction in the tunnel behind the vein to facilitate the entry of the stapler. After dividing the vein, a small abnormal branch is identified, and a vascular clip is applied using a 45-degree clip applier. The harmonic device is used on the distal end, and the bronchus is lifted. The mediastinal tissue and flora are removed before applying the stapler with green reload. The anesthetist inflates the lung to ensure no compromise to the upper or medial bronchus. The loop is freed and placed in a bag for retrieval from the chest.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy using a systemic nodal dissection approach, starting below the azygous vein to expose the lymph nodes. The section between the lymph nodes and the pulmonary artery is uplifted using a suction device. The surgeon frees the lymph nodes from beneath the azygous vein and proceeds between the azygous vein and the superior vena cava, opening the mediastinal pleura to access lymph node stations four and two.

The surgeon performs careful blunt dissection around the superior vena cava, targeting lymph node stations two and four in the right paratracheal region. The lung is retracted anteriorly using lung grasping forceps with a peanut, exposing the posterior hilum for dissection of station seven, the subcarinal lymph node. The harmonic device is used for dissection and suction, freeing lymph nodes before grasping them to avoid resistance. After dissecting station seven, the surgeon proceeds to station eleven, revealing the bronchial and arterial stumps. Hemostatic agents are applied to prevent bleeding at the lymph node dissection sites. A chest tube is placed, and the lung is inflated before closing the port.
